title: Compensator sprocket noise and failure — Twin Cam and Milwaukee-Eight
description: The compensator is a spring-loaded sprocket assembly in the primary that absorbs drivetrain shock from the big V-twin's power pulses. When it wears, you get a loud clunk or rattle on decel, cold start clatter, and eventually a metallic grinding as the springs and ramps wear through. Every Twin Cam and M8 does this eventually.
causes: Spring and ramp wear from normal use
Primary chain tension too tight loading compensator
fix_procedure: 1. Listen for clunk/rattle on deceleration or neutral-to-first engagement — if it's a single metallic clunk, compensator is suspect. 2. Remove primary cover and inspect compensator: look for worn ramps, broken springs, loose center bolt. 3. Replace entire compensator assembly — don't try to replace just the springs. 4. Check primary chain adjustment while the cover is off. 5. Refill with correct primary fluid (HD Formula+ or Spectro Heavy Duty). 6. Forum tip: the factory compensator on Twin Cams is garbage — it's designed to wear. Two upgrades dominate the forums: the Baker Grudge (lockout — eliminates the compensator entirely, transmits all shock to the drivetrain — rough but bulletproof) and the SE Screamin Eagle compensator (better springs and ramps, smoother, lasts 3x longer). For a daily rider, the SE compensator is the better choice. For a drag bike, Baker Grudge. Also, the M8 compensator is improved over the TC but still wears — just takes longer.
parts_needed: Compensator assembly (SE Screamin Eagle or Baker Grudge)

title: Intake manifold seal leak — all V-twins with shared manifold
description: Every Harley V-twin with a single-bore intake manifold (Evo through M8) is prone to intake seal leaks. The rubber manifold seals between the manifold and each cylinder head harden from heat cycling, allowing unmetered air into one or both cylinders. Symptoms: lean condition, idle surge, backfire, and lean-related DTCs on EFI models.
causes: Intake seal rubber hardening from heat cycling
Seal compression set (loses elasticity over time)
fix_procedure: 1. Spray test: engine idling, spray carb cleaner around each intake seal. If RPM changes (rises on carb, drops on EFI with O2 correction), that seal is leaking. 2. Remove air cleaner, throttle body/carb, and intake manifold. 3. Replace BOTH seals even if only one is leaking — the other is the same age. 4. Use James Gasket JGI-27062-95 or Cometic seals. 5. Apply thin coat of Hylomar Blue sealant to the seal OD before installing. 6. Install new clamps (don't reuse). Don't overtighten — finger-tight plus 1/4 turn. 7. Forum tip: intake seal leaks are the single most common driveability problem on Harleys. Do this job every 2 years or 20K miles as preventive maintenance — a $15 seal kit prevents a $200 tow. The spray test works every time. Also, when reinstalling, make sure the manifold is perfectly aligned between the heads — if it's cocked, the seals will pinch and leak immediately.
parts_needed: Intake manifold seal kit (James Gasket or Cometic)

title: Heat soak and hot restart issues — air-cooled V-twins
description: Air-cooled Harleys absorb enormous heat in traffic or at long idle, particularly the rear cylinder which gets less airflow. When you shut down a hot bike and try to restart after 10-20 minutes, the heat-soaked engine is harder to turn over, fuel can vapor-lock in the throttle body/carb, and the IAC (idle air control) may not compensate correctly.
causes: Fuel vapor lock from heat radiating into fuel rail
Battery voltage drop from heat (higher resistance)
IAC valve sticking from carbon buildup
fix_procedure: 1. If hot-restart fails, wait 5 minutes — let the underhood heat dissipate. 2. On EFI bikes: hold throttle wide open while cranking (clear flood mode on most ECMs). 3. On carb bikes: close the choke/enrichener fully and crack throttle 1/4 turn while cranking. 4. If chronic hot-start issue: check IAC valve for carbon buildup (clean with carb cleaner). 5. Check battery condition — a marginal battery fails in hot conditions because internal resistance increases with temperature. 6. Forum tip: heat soak is not a fault — it's physics. Air-cooled bikes in traffic get HOT. The rear cylinder runs 50-100F hotter than the front on most Harleys. The best mitigation is: don't park a hot bike and try to restart in 10-20 minutes (the worst window — engine heat is radiating into everything but the cylinders are losing compression heat). Either restart immediately or wait 20+ minutes. Also, Harley's factory oil cooler (on Touring models) helps significantly — if your bike doesn't have one, it's a worthwhile add for hot-climate riding.

title: Primary oil/fluid leak — derby cover and primary gasket
description: The primary cover on Big Twin Harleys develops leaks at the derby cover (inspection cover), primary gasket, and shifter shaft seal. On Sportsters with shared oil, a primary leak means engine oil is leaking too. These leaks are progressive — they start as a weep and become a drip.
causes: Primary cover gasket shrinkage from heat
Over-torqued primary cover bolts warping the cover
fix_procedure: 1. Identify leak location: clean primary cover completely, ride, then inspect for fresh oil. 2. Derby cover leak: replace O-ring ($2 part). Torque derby cover screws to 10 ft-lbs — no more. 3. Primary gasket leak: remove primary cover, scrape old gasket, install new gasket. Use James Gasket paper gasket (not silicone RTV). 4. Shifter shaft seal: pry out old seal, drive in new seal. 5. On reassembly, torque primary cover bolts in a cross pattern to spec. 6. Forum tip: 90% of primary leaks are the derby cover O-ring — a $2 fix that takes 5 minutes. The second most common is the shifter shaft seal — $5 part, 30 minutes, but you need to remove the primary cover. If the primary cover itself is warped from over-torquing, you can surface it on sandpaper on a flat surface (glass plate), but honestly a used cover from eBay is $30-50 and easier. NEVER use RTV sealant on the primary gasket — it chunks off inside and clogs the oil passages.

title: Clutch adjustment and clutch pack wear — cable and hydraulic
description: Harley clutches need periodic adjustment on cable models (Evo through most Twin Cams) and fluid service on hydraulic models (VRSC, late Softails, M8). A maladjusted or worn clutch causes slipping under load, dragging at stops (hard to find neutral), and notchy shifting. High-torque engines eat clutches faster than Japanese bikes.
causes: Friction plate wear from high torque
fix_procedure: 1. Cable clutch adjustment: at handlebar lever, adjust freeplay to 1/16-1/8 inch. Then adjust at the cable midpoint (if equipped) and finally at the clutch arm on the primary. 2. If properly adjusted and still slipping: the clutch pack is worn. Inspect friction plates, steel plates, and springs. 3. Replace friction plates and steels as a set. Upgrade springs to heavy-duty (Barnett or Energy One). 4. For hydraulic clutches: flush fluid every 2 years (DOT 4). Bleed system if spongy. 5. Forum tip: the #1 Harley clutch mistake is riding the friction zone too long at stops. Harley clutches are not designed for sustained slip — they're designed for quick engagement. If you're lugging through a parking lot in the friction zone for 30 seconds, you're cooking the clutch. Also, for Big Twins with performance upgrades (cams, bore, tune), upgrade to an aftermarket clutch pack — the stock clutch can't hold the extra torque. Barnett Scorpion and Energy One Extra are the go-to choices.
parts_needed: Clutch friction plates + steel plates kit
Heavy-duty clutch springs (Barnett or Energy One)

title: Rear suspension sag and shock degradation
description: Harley rear shocks (especially on Sportsters and Dynas) are notoriously cheap from the factory. They sag under load, bottom out on bumps, and lose damping within 15K-20K miles. Two-up riding accelerates the problem. The result: poor handling, rear-end wallowing, and accelerated tire wear.
causes: Factory shock springs too soft for rider weight
Shock oil degradation and seal leakage
Never serviced or adjusted since new
fix_procedure: 1. Check rear sag: with rider seated, measure shock length. Compare to unloaded. More than 30% compression = shocks are too soft or worn. 2. Check for oil weeping on shock body — if wet, shock seals are gone. 3. On Touring models with air shocks: check air pressure (35-50 psi depending on load) and inflate. 4. On twin-shock models (Sportster, Dyna): replace as a pair. 5. Aftermarket shocks: Progressive 412/444, Ohlins HD 044, Fox. 6. Forum tip: upgrading shocks is the single best handling improvement you can make on a Harley — more than tires, more than bars, more than anything. The factory shocks on a Sportster are $15 parts — they're designed for a 150 lb rider on smooth roads. Progressive 412 series ($200-300/pair) transform the bike. For Touring models, the stock air shock is adequate but the Ohlins or Legend Revo are a generation better. Also, when installing new shocks, set preload for your riding weight + gear — don't just bolt them on at factory preload.
parts_needed: Rear shock set (Progressive 412/444, Ohlins, or Fox)

title: Wheel bearing failure — front and rear
description: Harley wheel bearings (sealed ball bearings) wear from mileage, water exposure, and loads. Symptoms: humming or grinding noise that changes with speed (not RPM), lateral wheel play when grabbed at top and bottom, and ABS light on 2008+ models (the ABS tone ring is on the bearing seal).
causes: ABS tone ring corrosion affecting bearing seal
fix_procedure: 1. Check: wheel off the ground, grab at 12 and 6 o'clock and rock. Any play = bearing worn. 2. Spin the wheel — listen for grinding or roughness. A good bearing spins silently. 3. To replace: remove wheel, remove axle spacers, press out old bearings, press in new ones. 4. ALWAYS replace both bearings in the wheel (inner and outer) even if only one is bad. 5. On ABS-equipped bikes: the rear bearing has an integrated ABS tone ring — use the correct HD bearing to avoid ABS faults. 6. Forum tip: Harley wheel bearings are standard sealed bearings — you can buy the exact same bearing from a bearing supply house for 1/3 the HD price. The numbers are usually stamped on the bearing outer race. However, on ABS bikes (2008+), the rear bearing with the ABS tone ring MUST be the HD part — aftermarket bearings don't have the correct tone ring spacing and you'll get ABS faults. Also, when pressing bearings, always press on the outer race (not the inner) to avoid destroying the new bearing.
parts_needed: Wheel bearing set (inner + outer, per wheel)

title: Exhaust leak at head pipe flange — all models
description: Exhaust leaks at the cylinder head flanges are universal on Harleys. The exhaust nuts loosen from thermal cycling (expand hot, contract cold), and the exhaust gaskets crush and fail. Symptoms: ticking noise on cold start that fades when hot (metal expands and seals), popping on decel, and lean codes on EFI bikes.
causes: Exhaust flange nut loosening from thermal cycling
fix_procedure: 1. Cold start the bike and listen at each exhaust port — a tick or hiss that goes away when warm is a classic exhaust leak. 2. Check exhaust flange nut torque — spec is usually 10-15 ft-lbs depending on model. 3. If nuts are loose, tighten. If still leaking, the gaskets are crushed. 4. Remove exhaust, replace gaskets (Cometic or James Gasket spiral-wound are best). 5. New flange nuts (some are one-time-use serrated flanged nuts). 6. Torque evenly — snug both before final torque on either. 7. Forum tip: don't use anti-seize on exhaust flange nuts at the head — the heat will cook the anti-seize and the nuts will actually seize worse. Instead, use a thin coat of copper spray on the gasket face. Also, check your exhaust flanges for flat contact — a warped flange will never seal no matter how many gaskets you throw at it. Lay the flange on a piece of glass with sandpaper and lap it flat if needed.

title: Tire wear pattern diagnosis — cupping, feathering, flat-spotting
description: Abnormal tire wear on Harleys tells you about suspension, alignment, and riding habit problems. Cupping (scalloped wear) = worn shocks. Center-only wear = overinflation or all-highway riding. Edge wear = underinflation. Flat spots = hard braking. Mechanics should read tires before replacing them.
causes: Worn rear shocks (causes cupping on rear tire)
fix_procedure: 1. Read the wear pattern BEFORE replacing the tire — it's diagnostic information. 2. Cupping (scalloped patches): worn shocks — replace shocks before new tire or it'll cup again in 3K miles. 3. Center strip: overinflation or all-highway. Reduce pressure to spec and/or add more lean angle (cornering). 4. Edge wear: underinflation. Check and maintain correct pressure (front 36 psi, rear 40 psi typical — check door placard). 5. One-sided wear: wheel misalignment or belt/chain tension uneven side to side. 6. Flat spots: check ABS function, brake caliper for drag. 7. Forum tip: the #1 cause of premature rear tire wear on touring Harleys is worn rear shocks. The shock bounces the tire off the road surface and the tire scrubs instead of rolling — this causes the cupping pattern. New tires on old shocks = 5K miles. New tires on new shocks = 15K miles. Also, Harley tires run at higher pressures than you'd expect — 40 psi rear is normal. Don't drop to 30 psi because it 'feels softer' — you'll destroy the tire in 3K miles.

title: Fuel quality and ethanol damage — carb and EFI eras
description: Modern E10 (10% ethanol) fuel damages carburetors, fuel lines, and fuel system seals on older Harleys. Even on EFI bikes, ethanol attracts moisture that causes corrosion in the fuel tank, injectors, and fuel pump. Bikes that sit for >30 days are especially vulnerable.
causes: Ethanol absorbing moisture from air (phase separation)
Ethanol degrading rubber fuel lines and seals
Stale fuel after 30+ days of sitting
Carburetor varnish from evaporated ethanol fuel
fix_procedure: 1. If bike sat >30 days: drain old fuel, refill with fresh premium. 2. On carb bikes: if hard starting after sitting, the carb jets are probably varnished. Remove float bowl, clean jets with carb cleaner and compressed air. 3. On EFI bikes: stale fuel clogs injectors — run a tank of Techron or SeaFoam through the system. 4. For long-term storage (>30 days): fill tank completely (less air = less moisture), add fuel stabilizer (Sta-Bil), and run the engine for 5 minutes to circulate treated fuel. 5. Forum tip: ethanol-free fuel (E0) is available at many stations and is ALWAYS the better choice for motorcycles. Use pure-gas.org to find E0 stations near you. If E0 isn't available, use premium (91+ octane, usually has less ethanol) and add Sta-Bil Marine formula on every fill-up — it's designed to fight ethanol moisture absorption. For carb bikes that sit, E10 fuel will varnish jets in as little as 3 weeks. The best prevention is riding the bike — fuel that's being burned can't go stale.
parts_needed: Fuel stabilizer (Sta-Bil Marine or Sea Foam)
